title: Single-Collection Design & Multi-Value Indexes
description: The universal NoSQL principle — what is accessed together should be stored together — refined with compound indexes, multi-value indexes, and the full power of SQL analytics.
introduction:
There is a universal principle in data modeling that predates any specific database: what is accessed together should be stored together. In relational systems, normalization optimizes for storage efficiency and update consistency — and then you pay for it at read time with multi-table joins. In document systems, you have a choice. You can embed related data inside a single document for fast reads, or you can store related documents separately in the same collection and use indexes to collect them.

That choice is not a philosophy decision. It is a physics decision. The right answer depends on your storage engine, your access patterns, and the size of your documents.

Consider the tradeoffs. Embedding an advisor's entire client roster inside a single advisor document gives you one-read access to the full entity graph. But when a client's portfolio value changes — which happens constantly in financial services — you are rewriting the entire advisor document for a single field update. If that document is 2KB, the overhead is negligible. If it is 200KB, you are moving 200KB of data for a 8-byte number change. On MongoDB, WiredTiger writes 32KB leaf pages regardless of how much data changed within them. On Oracle, documents under roughly 8KB are stored inline with the row in the table's data blocks. Cross that threshold and the document migrates to LOB storage — a fundamentally different I/O path with different performance characteristics. These are the physics of your storage engine, and they determine whether embedding or separating is the right model.

Single-collection design refines the universal principle by taking these physics into account. Instead of embedding everything into one massive document, you store small, focused documents — each representing a single entity like an advisor profile, a client summary, or a holding — together in the same collection. Compound indexes on shared attributes group them, so a single indexed query retrieves the entire related set in one round trip. You get the locality benefit of embedding without the write amplification penalty. And because each document is small, you stay within your storage engine's sweet spot — inline storage on Oracle, efficient page utilization on MongoDB.

[svg:m3-single-table.svg]

The collection in this module — advisory_entities — stores advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions as separate documents with natural domain attributes. Advisor and client documents share an advisorId attribute, so a single indexed query retrieves an advisor with all their clients. Holding and transaction documents share an accountId, grouping them by account. Every holding carries a symbol attribute, enabling cross-account lookups. No artificial key encoding. No concatenated prefixes. Just natural attributes with compound indexes — the same pattern that works on any document database.

This is where Oracle transforms the pattern. On a pure document database, single-collection design gives you fast point reads and bounded range scans within an index prefix. That is all. Cross-group analytics require application-side aggregation. Joining entity types requires multiple queries stitched together in code. On Oracle, you keep the locality benefits but gain the full SQL engine: joins across entity types within the collection, GROUP BY and window functions for cross-group analytics, CTEs for multi-step computations, and JSON_TABLE for shredding arrays into relational rows. And you get CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX — full B-tree indexes on every element inside a JSON array, queryable individually or combined. MongoDB's parallel arrays restriction prohibits compound indexes on two array fields. Oracle has no such limitation.

By the end of this module, you will have explored the entity collection, created compound indexes for multiple access patterns, built multi-value indexes on arrays and combined them in queries that other document databases physically cannot execute, and run SQL analytics that would require a separate analytical database on any other platform. The universal principle, refined by storage engine physics, amplified by SQL convergence.
checkpoint: One collection. Four entity types. Natural attributes with compound indexes. SQL joins, aggregation, and multi-value indexes — all on the same collection.

exercises.title: Explore the Entity Collection
exercises.description: See how multiple entity types co-exist in one collection, query by shared attributes, and filter by entity type.
exercises.explanation:
This is single-collection design in practice. Four entity types — advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions — live together in the advisory_entities collection. Each document uses **natural domain attributes** for grouping. Advisor and client documents share **advisorId** — querying `WHERE data.advisorId.number() = 5` retrieves the advisor profile and all their clients in one scan. Holding and transaction documents share **accountId** for account-level grouping. Every holding and transaction carries a **symbol** for cross-account lookups.

There are no artificial key encodings, no concatenated prefixes. The **entityType** field discriminates between advisors, clients, holdings, and transactions. You can count by type, filter within a group, or aggregate across the entire collection. On Oracle, the cost-based optimizer treats these JSON path expressions exactly like relational column predicates, choosing index scans or full scans based on data statistics.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT DISTINCT ae.data.advisorId.number() AS advisor_id FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() IS NOT NULL ORDER BY 1` — see all advisor IDs in the collection
- `SELECT ae.data.entityType.string() AS type, json_serialize(ae.data PRETTY) AS doc FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.advisorId.number() = 3 AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'client'` — explore a different advisor's clients
exercises.code:
-- See all entity types in one collection
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

-- Query by shared attribute — get an advisor and all their clients
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       json_serialize(data PRETTY) AS document
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.advisorId.number() = 5
ORDER BY data.entityType.string();

-- Filter by entity type — get just the clients for an advisor
SELECT data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.advisorId.number() = 5
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'client'
ORDER BY data.totalValue.number() DESC;

steps.label: Count all entity types in the collection
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS entity_type,
       COUNT(*) AS count
FROM advisory_entities
GROUP BY data.entityType.string()
ORDER BY count DESC;

steps.label: Query by advisorId — get an advisor and all their clients
steps.code:
SELECT data.entityType.string() AS type,
       json_serialize(data PRETTY) AS document
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.advisorId.number() = 5
ORDER BY data.entityType.string();

steps.label: Filter by entity type — get just the clients for an advisor
steps.code:
SELECT data.firstName.string() || ' ' || data.lastName.string() AS client_name,
       data.riskProfile.string() AS risk_profile,
       data.totalValue.number() AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.advisorId.number() = 5
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'client'
ORDER BY data.totalValue.number() DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities WHERE json_value(data, '$.entityType') IS NOT NULL
exercises.checkpoint: You just queried a single collection containing four entity types using natural domain attributes. One query on advisorId retrieves both the advisor and their clients — the universal principle in action.

exercises.title: Compound Indexes for Access Patterns
exercises.description: Create indexes on natural attributes to support advisor, account, and symbol access patterns.
exercises.explanation:
Every access pattern your application needs should resolve to an indexed lookup — a point read or a bounded range scan. The index on **advisorId** supports the advisor-centric pattern: retrieve an advisor and all their clients. The index on **accountId** supports the account-centric pattern: retrieve all holdings and transactions for an account. The index on **symbol** enables a cross-cutting access pattern: find every holding or transaction for a given stock symbol across all accounts.

This is where Oracle's approach differs fundamentally from databases that require you to encode access patterns into key structures. You do not need to design composite key strings or overload index attributes. You simply create an index on the attribute you want to query. The cost-based optimizer decides when and how to use each index, and can combine multiple indexes in a single query plan using bitmap conversion. There is no arbitrary limit on the number of indexes, no replication lag, and no storage duplication beyond the B-tree itself.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.symbol.string() AS symbol, COUNT(*) AS holdings FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY ae.data.symbol.string() ORDER BY 2 DESC` — see which symbols appear most frequently across all accounts

steps.label: Create index on advisorId for advisor + client lookups
Create index on accountId for holdings + transaction lookups
Create index on symbol for cross-account lookups
Use the symbol index to find all AAPL holdings
steps.code:
SELECT data.accountId.number() AS account_id,
       data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities
WHERE data.symbol.string() = 'AAPL'
  AND data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_ADVISOR', 'IDX_ACCOUNT', 'IDX_SYMBOL')
exercises.checkpoint: You just created three indexes on natural domain attributes — advisorId, accountId, symbol. Each one enables a different access pattern. No artificial key encoding. No index limits. No replication lag.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Indexes — Index Into Arrays
exercises.description: Create multi-value indexes on tags and sectors arrays, then query across both — something impossible on MongoDB due to the parallel arrays restriction.
exercises.explanation:
**CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX** lets Oracle build a B-tree index on every element inside a JSON array. Each array element gets its own index entry, so `JSON_EXISTS` queries on array contents resolve via fast B-tree lookup — not a full collection scan. This is critical for any domain with tags, categories, or multi-valued attributes.

Here is where document modeling tradeoffs become concrete. Tags and sectors are relatively static metadata — they describe the nature of a holding, not its current state. Embedding them inside the holding document is the right call because it avoids a join on every read, and the multi-value index makes each array element individually queryable. If these tags changed frequently, you might store them as separate documents in the collection and join on demand — trading read overhead for write efficiency. The right answer always depends on the read/write ratio and the storage engine physics.

The critical advantage: MongoDB prohibits compound indexes on two array fields — the **parallel arrays restriction**. Oracle has no such limitation. You can create multi-value indexes on tags AND sectors and query both in the same WHERE clause. The optimizer uses **bitmap conversion** to intersect the two indexes efficiently.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT ae.data.symbol.string(), json_serialize(ae.data.tags) FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — find growth-tagged holdings and see their full tag sets
exercises.code:
-- Create a multi-value index on the tags array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.tags.string());

-- Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.sectors.string());

-- Find all holdings tagged "dividend"
SELECT data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

-- Combine: find all S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
SELECT data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the tags array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_tags ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.tags.string());

steps.label: Create a multi-value index on the sectors array
steps.code:
CREATE MULTIVALUE INDEX idx_sectors ON advisory_entities ae
    (ae.data.sectors.string());

steps.label: Find all holdings tagged 'dividend'
steps.code:
SELECT data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.quantity.number() AS quantity,
       data.marketValue.number() AS market_value,
       json_serialize(data.tags) AS tags
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

steps.label: Combine both indexes — find S&P 500 holdings in the technology sector
steps.code:
SELECT data.symbol.string() AS symbol,
       data.marketValue.number() AS market_value
FROM advisory_entities ae
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "sp500")')
  AND JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.sectors?(@ == "technology")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM user_indexes WHERE index_name IN ('IDX_TAGS', 'IDX_SECTORS')
exercises.checkpoint: You just indexed into JSON arrays with MULTIVALUE INDEX and queried across two array fields in one WHERE clause. On MongoDB, this compound index would fail — parallel arrays restriction. On Oracle, it's standard B-tree semantics.

exercises.title: SQL Analytics on Collection Data
exercises.description: Cross-entity aggregation, tag analytics, and sector exposure reports — queries that pure document databases cannot optimize.
exercises.explanation:
This is where the convergence advantage becomes undeniable. **JSON_TABLE** shreds JSON arrays into relational rows using a COLUMNS clause that maps JSON paths to typed SQL columns, turning nested array elements into first-class rows that participate in GROUP BY, SUM, window functions, and CTEs. The tag analytics query explodes each holding's tags array into individual rows, then groups and aggregates across the entire book of business. On a pure document database, this requires a multi-stage aggregation pipeline with no cost-based optimization.

The sector exposure report goes further: it uses a **CTE (Common Table Expression)** with `SUM() OVER()` as a window function to calculate each sector's percentage of total portfolio value in a single pass. This is exactly why single-collection design is more powerful on Oracle — you model as documents for developer velocity and storage efficiency, then query as relational for analytical power. The optimizer figures out the fastest execution path.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT jt.tag, ae.data.accountId.number() AS account, COUNT(*) FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag, ae.data.accountId.number() ORDER BY 1, 2` — see tag distribution per account
exercises.code:
-- TAG ANALYTICS: Holdings count by tag (across entire book)
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

-- SECTOR EXPOSURE REPORT: Market value by sector with percentage
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

steps.label: Tag analytics — holdings count and total value by tag
steps.code:
SELECT jt.tag,
       COUNT(*) AS holding_count,
       SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS total_value
FROM advisory_entities ae,
JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
GROUP BY jt.tag
ORDER BY total_value DESC;

steps.label: Sector exposure report — market value by sector with portfolio percentage
steps.code:
WITH sector_values AS (
    SELECT jt.sector,
           SUM(ae.data.marketValue.number()) AS sector_value
    FROM advisory_entities ae,
    JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.sectors[*]' COLUMNS (sector VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt
    WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
    GROUP BY jt.sector
)
SELECT sector,
       sector_value,
       ROUND(sector_value / SUM(sector_value) OVER () * 100, 1) AS pct_of_portfolio
FROM sector_values
ORDER BY sector_value DESC;

exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM (SELECT jt.tag FROM advisory_entities ae, JSON_TABLE(ae.data, '$.tags[*]' COLUMNS (tag VARCHAR2(50) PATH '$')) jt WHERE ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding' GROUP BY jt.tag)
exercises.checkpoint: You just ran SQL aggregations across entity types using JSON_TABLE and window functions — the analytical power of SQL applied directly to document collection data.

exercises.title: Multi-Value Index + Duality View — Convergence
exercises.description: Combine single-collection design with duality views — multi-value index identifies matches, SQL JOIN navigates relationships, duality view shapes the output.
exercises.explanation:
This is the convergence payoff — **three paradigms in one query**, one optimizer, one round trip. The multi-value index on the entity collection identifies holdings tagged 'dividend' via B-tree lookup, avoiding any collection scan. The SQL JOIN navigates from the collection entity to the relational accounts table using the natural **accountId** attribute — no string parsing, no key decoding. The duality view projects the final output as a rich JSON document with nested accounts and holdings — the shape your API consumer expects.

On a polyglot architecture, this query would require three separate systems: a document store for the entity collection, a relational database for client and account data, and an aggregation layer to stitch them together. Here it is one SQL statement, and the cost-based optimizer decides whether to use nested loop joins, hash joins, or merge joins based on real-time statistics.

This pattern is the architectural thesis of the entire workshop: **model the domain, project the access, let the optimizer handle the physics**.

**Try this in the editor:**
- `SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio FROM advisory_entities ae JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = ae.data.accountId.number() JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "growth")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'` — change the tag filter to 'growth' to see different client portfolios
exercises.code:
-- Find clients whose holdings include "dividend" tagged securities
-- using the multi-value index on the entity collection,
-- then join to the relational model for the full client document
SELECT json_serialize(cp.data PRETTY) AS client_portfolio
FROM advisory_entities ae
JOIN accounts a ON a.account_id = ae.data.accountId.number()
JOIN client_portfolio_dv cp ON json_value(cp.data, '$._id' RETURNING NUMBER) = a.client_id
WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")')
  AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding';
exercises.validationQuery: SELECT COUNT(*) AS cnt FROM advisory_entities ae WHERE JSON_EXISTS(ae.data, '$.tags?(@ == "dividend")') AND ae.data.entityType.string() = 'holding'
exercises.checkpoint: You just combined multi-value index lookup, SQL JOIN on a natural attribute, and duality view projection in a single query. Three paradigms. One optimizer. One round trip.
